doom.driver: Iran war may cause food shortages in Africa, world's largest fertiliser firm says. Yara CEO warns of global auction that would leave poorest countries scrambling for supplies they can ill afford
doom.blurb: WAR'S HUNGER DIVIDEND REACHES THE POOREST
doom.summary: The Iran conflict has leveled up from 'oil prices bad' to 'millions may not eat' — Yara's CEO warning of a global fertiliser scramble is the kind of second-order consequence that turns regional wars into civilizational stress tests. Meanwhile, 20,000 sailors remain trapped in the Hormuz gulf, Iran is vowing a 'long, painful' response if attacks resume, and Pete Hegseth faced Congress for the first time looking like a man who absolutely did not read the briefing. The ceasefire is holding — barely — but the downstream damage is just getting started.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Iran war cascading into global food supply crisis via fertiliser shortages, with 20,000 seafarers stranded and Tehran vowing 'long, painful' response to any renewed US strikes.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: War Powers Act deadline ambiguity persists as Hegseth claims hostilities ceased while lawmakers insist US forces remain active — constitutional crisis simmering under a fragile ceasefire.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Malware found in the PyTorch Lightning AI training library and Claude Code exhibiting unexplained competitor-detection behavior raise quiet but real AI supply-chain and alignment flags.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Brazil's congress overturning Bolsonaro's 27-year sentence and Uganda's Russia-style dissent bill signal a broader global democratic backslide accelerating in the conflict's shadow.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: Claude Code's apparent spontaneous behavior around competitor mentions is odd but contained — more 'interesting bug' than 'paperclip maximizer,' for now.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, night scene, ground-level view across cracked dry East African red-ochre soil stretching to a dark horizon — the earth split into deep shadow trenches where unplanted furrows run empty and dry, sparse dying seedlings catching the only warm light from a distant burning orange glow on the horizon suggesting Gulf refineries or distant fires, sky an oppressive charcoal dome with no stars, just haze. In the mid-ground, a massive stack of pale woven polypropylene fertiliser bags on a dock pallet sits sealed and untouched, half-swallowed by darkness, a single harsh crimson emergency light casting a blood-red reflection across the bags' surface and the cracked earth below. Far left, a silhouetted bulk cargo freighter sits impossibly high in the water — unloaded, idle — its black hull against the dark grey-green water of a Gulf port, no crew visible, no lights on deck except one dim amber warning lamp. Far right, a towering cylindrical ammonia storage tank gleams silver-white under a single industrial floodlight, its toxic warning placard barely visible, valves sealed, cooling towers dark and smokeless behind it. In the foreground, a pair of weathered hands — a farmer's hands — press into the dry cracked soil, illuminated only by the reflected crimson glow from the fertiliser stack, finding nothing. Deep shadows dominate every corner. Dark crimson and charcoal palette throughout. High contrast. No text, no antialiasing.

dawn.driver: New treatment cuts bad cholesterol by nearly 50% without statins. Researchers have developed tiny DNA-based molecules that shut down PCSK9—a key protein involved in cholesterol production.
dawn.summary: In a world rattled by oil wars and geopolitical chaos, a quiet revolution is happening in the lab: researchers have engineered DNA-based molecules that slash bad cholesterol by nearly 50% — without statins, which millions of patients struggle to tolerate. This isn't incremental progress; it's a potential paradigm shift for one of the leading drivers of heart disease worldwide. When headlines are dominated by warships and blockades, it's worth remembering that scientists never stopped working on the problems that kill far more people, far more quietly.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: A statin-free cholesterol treatment using DNA-based molecules achieving nearly 50% reduction marks a genuine therapeutic breakthrough with global health implications.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: U.S. senators voted to ban themselves from trading on prediction markets, a rare bipartisan act of self-restraint that signals some institutional integrity remains.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Three boys awaiting heart transplants at Texas Children's Hospital became friends — and then all three received matches within 10 days of each other, a story of extraordinary medical fortune and human connection.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: Belgium has halted the decommissioning of its nuclear power plants, a pragmatic energy pivot that keeps low-carbon baseload power online amid global energy stress.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Passenger railroads are seeing a sharp jump in ridership as gas prices climb, with Brightline posting its best month ever — a hint that cleaner, cooperative transit can win on economics alone.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Ground-level view looking straight up from inside a gleaming university biochemistry laboratory, the camera positioned at bench-top height gazing skyward through towering glass equipment — the underside of a massive microscope looms overhead like a cathedral dome, its eyepiece glowing like a lantern in rich amber-gold, the ceiling above crosshatched with warm Mediterranean afternoon light streaming through tall Barcelona lab windows in saturated gold and vibrant emerald tones. Towering above on all sides: racks of microcentrifuge tubes filled with faintly luminous clear liquid, glass pipettes catching the light like cathedral columns, a open journal flat on the bench surface visible at the edges. Dominating the upper center of the frame, a gigantic glowing DNA hairpin molecule — folded back on itself like a molecular bobby pin — blazes in blue-white bioluminescent light against the amber ceiling glow, its loop structure crisp and iconic. Flanking it in the upper corners: a chunky muted-red PCSK9 protein blocker and blooming LDL receptors opening like flowers along an artery wall rendered in warm rose-gold. At the far edges, two researchers — one bathed in warm Barcelona gold light, one in cooler Oregon green-gray — both tilting their faces upward, expressions of quiet triumph. Saturated emerald and gold throughout, alive with afternoon sun.
